title: W7 C Implicit Function Theorem
body:
Mathematical Economics

Juan Carlos Carbajal

School of Economics UNSW–Sydney

Week 7C — Term 1, 2026

1/7  Implicit Function Theorem

2/7   Setting Up the Scene

Let F : R2 → R be continuously differentiable and consider the equation

F (x1 , x2 ) = 0. (1)

This equation defines a curve (level set) in the (x1 , x2 )–plane.

3/7   Setting Up the Scene

Let F : R2 → R be continuously differentiable and consider the equation

F (x1 , x2 ) = 0. (1)

This equation defines a curve (level set) in the (x1 , x2 )–plane.

Local Solvability ◦ Suppose that x̂ = (x̂1 , x̂2 ) solves Eq. (1). ◦ If Eq. (1) is “non-degenerate” in x2 at x̂ = (x̂1 , x̂2 ), then around x̂1 we can solve it uniquely for x2 as a function of x1 . ◦ In other words, there exists a function φ such that for all x1 near x̂1 :

x2 = φ(x1 ) and F (x1 , φ(x1 )) = 0.

3/7   Implicit Function Theorem (IFT)

A convenient “non-degeneracy” condition is ∂F (x̂1 , x̂2 ) ̸= 0. ∂x2

◦ Intuition: the level set F (x1 , x2 ) = 0 is not locally “vertical”. ◦ Changing x2 actually moves you off the level set.

4/7   Implicit Function Theorem (IFT)

A convenient “non-degeneracy” condition is ∂F (x̂1 , x̂2 ) ̸= 0. ∂x2

◦ Intuition: the level set F (x1 , x2 ) = 0 is not locally “vertical”. ◦ Changing x2 actually moves you off the level set.

Implicit Function Theorem — main takeaway:

When x2 = φ(x1 ) is defined implicitly by F (x1 , x2 ) = 0, we can compute

∂F (x1 , φ(x1 ))/∂x1 Dφ(x1 ) = − . ∂F (x1 , φ(x1 ))/∂x2

In particular, at (x̂1 , x̂2 ),

dx2 Fx (x̂1 , x̂2 ) = − 1 . dx1 (x̂1 ,x̂2 ) Fx2 (x̂1 , x̂2 )

4/7   IFT Intuition: Why Does it Work

◦ Along the implicitly-defined curve x2 = φ(x1 ), we have

0 = F (x1 , φ(x1 )).

◦ Differentiate both sides with respect to x1 (chain rule):

∂F (x1 , φ(x1 )) ∂F (x1 , φ(x1 )) 0 = + Dφ(x1 ). ∂x1 ∂x2

◦ If ∂F/∂x2 ̸= 0, rearranging obtains

∂F (x1 , φ(x1 )) / ∂x1 Dφ(x1 ) = − . ∂F (x1 , φ(x1 )) / ∂x2

5/7   IFT Intuition: Why Does it Work

◦ Along the implicitly-defined curve x2 = φ(x1 ), we have

0 = F (x1 , φ(x1 )).

◦ Differentiate both sides with respect to x1 (chain rule):

∂F (x1 , φ(x1 )) ∂F (x1 , φ(x1 )) 0 = + Dφ(x1 ). ∂x1 ∂x2

◦ If ∂F/∂x2 ̸= 0, rearranging obtains

∂F (x1 , φ(x1 )) / ∂x1 Dφ(x1 ) = − . ∂F (x1 , φ(x1 )) / ∂x2

Interpretation To keep F fixed at zero, any small change in x1 must be offset by a change in x2 . The size of this adjustment is governed by the ratio of partial derivatives.

5/7   Application: Consumer Theory

Indifference Curve as an Implicit Equation ◦ Let u : R2+ → R be a continuously differentiable utility function.

◦ Fix a utility level ū and define

F (x1 , x2 ) := u(x1 , x2 ) − ū.

◦ The indifference curve is the set of consumption bundles x = (x1 , x2 ) ≥ 0 satisfying

F (x1 , x2 ) = 0 ⇐⇒ u(x1 , x2 ) = ū.

IFT condition ◦ If at bundle x̂ = (x̂1 , x̂2 ) on the indifference curve we have ∂F (x̂1 , x̂2 ) ∂u = (x̂1 , x̂2 ) ̸= 0, ∂x2 ∂x2

then locally, near x̂1 , we can write x2 = φ(x1 ).

6/7   Application: Consumer Theory

◦ The IFT gives us Fx1 (x1 , φ(x1 )) ∂u(x1 , φ(x1 ))/∂x1 Dφ(x1 ) = − = − . Fx2 (x1 , φ(x1 )) ∂u(x1 , φ(x1 ))/∂x2 ∂u ◦ In consumer theory, M Ui (x) = ∂xi (x). Thus we obtain the expression

dx2 M U1 (x) = − = − MRS12 (x) dx1 M U2 (x)

Economic interpretation ◦ The slope of the indifference curve at x is −MRS12 (x). ◦ When both goods are desirable, the slope is negative: to keep a constant utility, an increase in x1 must be offset by a decrease in x2 .

7/7
